Professor carbonic was diligently1 at work in his spacious2 laboratory analyzing, mixing and experimenting. He had been employed for more than fifteen years in the same pursuit of happiness, in the same house, same laboratory, and attended by the same servant woman, who in her long period of service had attained the plumpness and respectability of two hundred and ninety pounds.“Mag Nesia,” called the professor. The servant's name was Maggie Nesia—Professor Carbonic had contracted the title to save time, for in fifteen years he had not mounted the heights of greatness; he must work harder and faster as life is short, and eliminate such shameful waste of time as putting the "gie" on Maggie.“Mag Nesia!” the professor repeated.The old woman rolled slowly into the room."Get rid of these and bring the one the boy brought today."diligently: carefullyspacious: very bigHe handed her a tray containing three dead rats, whose brains had been subjected to analysis.“Yes, Marse,” answered Mag Nesia in a tone like citrate.The professor busied himself with a new preparation of zinc oxide and copper sulphate and sal ammoniac, his latest concoction3, which was about to 3. concoction: mixture of several be used and, like its predecessors, to be abandoned. things or featuresMag Nesia appeared bringing another rat, dead. The professor made no experiments on live animals. He had hired a boy in the neighbourhood to bring him fresh dead rats at twentyfive cents per head.Taking the tray he prepared a hypodermic filled with the new preparation. Carefully he made an incision4 above the right eye of the carcass through the bone. He lifted the hypodermic, half hopelessly, half expectantly. The old woman watched him, as she had done many times before, with always the same pitiful expression. Mag Nesia seldom expressed her views.Inserting the hypodermic needle and injecting the contents of the syringe, Professor Carbonic stepped back. Prof. Carbonic Makes a Great Discovery!“Great Saints!” His voice could be heard a mile. Slowly the rat's tail began to point skyward; and as slowly Mag Nesia began to turn white. Professor Carbonic stood as paralyzed. The rat trembled and moved his feet. The man of sixty years made one jump with the alacrity5 of a boy of sixteen; he grabbed the enlivened animal, and held it high above his head as he jumped about the room.The India BookmarkAncient Indian craftsmen produced a special steel called Wootz Steel. Its quality was so highly valued that it was exported to many countries and later used to make the famous Damascus swords.Spying the servant, who until now had seemed unable to move, he threw both arms around her, bringing the rat close to her face. Around the laboratory they danced to the tune of the woman's shrieks. The professor held on, and the woman yelled. Up and down spasmodically6 on the laboratory floor came the 4. incision: cut two hundred and ninety pounds with the professor thrown in. 5. alacrity: quickness6. spasmodically: caused by Bottles tumbled from the shelves. Furniture was upset. Precious 	spasm of muscleliquids flowed unrestrained and unnoticed. Finally the professor dropped with exhaustion and the rat and Mag Nesia made a dash for freedom.Professor Carbonic early in the morning betook himself to the nearest hardware store and purchased the tools necessary for his new profession. He was an M.D. and his recently acquired knowledge put him in a position to startle7 the world. Things were developing fast. Mag Nesia met him at the door and told him that Sally Soda, who was known to the neighbourhood as Sal or Sal Soda generally, had fallen down two flights of stairs, and Sal Soda's mother had read the elaborate sign of the new enemy of death, and begged that he come to see Sal as soon as he returned.  Bidding Mag Nesia to accompany him, he went to the laboratory and secured his precious preparation. Professor Carbonic and the unwilling Mag Nesia started out to put new life into a little Sal Soda who lived in the same block.Reaching the house they met the family physician then attendant on little Sal. Doctor X. Ray had also read the sign of the professor and his greeting was very chilly. 7. startle: surprise “How is the child?” asked the professor.“Fatally hurt and can live but an hour.” Then he added, “I have done all that can be done.” “All that you can do,” corrected the professor.With a withering glance, Doctor X. Ray left the room and the house. His reputation was such as to admit no intrusion.“I am sorry she is not dead, it would be easier to work, and also a more reasonable charge.” Giving Mag Nesia his instruments, he administered a local anesthetic; this done he selected a brace and bit that he had procured that morning. With these instruments he bored a small hole into the child's head. Inserting his hypodermic needle, he injected the immortal fluid, then cutting the end off a dowel8, which he had also procured that morning, he hammered it into the hole until it wedged itself tight. Professor Carbonic seated himself comfortably and awaited the action of his injection, while the plump Mag Nesia paced or rather waddled9 the floor with a bag of carpenter's tools under her arm. 8. dowel: a rod or a pinThe fluid worked. The child came to life and sat up.	9. waddled: to walk in an unsteady manner“It will be one dollar and twenty-five cents, Mrs. Soda,” apologized the professor. “I have to make that charge as it is so inconvenient to work on them when they are still alive.” Having collected his fee, the professor and Mag Nesia departed, amid the ever rising blessings of the Soda family. At 3:30 P.M. Mag Nesia sought her employer, who was asleep in the sitting room. “Marse Paul, a gentleman to see you.”The professor awoke and had her send the man in.The man entered hurriedly, hat in hand. “Are you Professor Carbonic?”“I am. What can I do for you?”“Can you…….?” the man hesitated. “My friend has just been killed in an accident. You couldn't…” he sounded skeptical10.“I know that it is unbelievable,” answered the professor. “But I can.”Professor Carbonic, for some years, had suffered from the effects of a weak heart. His fears on this score had recently been entirely relieved. He now had the prescription—Death no more! The startling discovery, and the happenings of the last twenty-four hours had begun to take effect on him, and he did not wish to make another call until he was feeling better.“I'll go,” said the professor after a period of musing11. “My discoveries are for the benefit of the human race, I must not consider myself.”He satisfied himself that he had all his tools. He had just sufficient preparation for one injection; this, he thought, would be enough; however, he placed in his case, two vials of different solutions, which were the basis of his discovery. These fluids had to be mixed, and after the chemical reaction had taken place, the preparation was ready for use.He searched the house for Mag Nesia, but the old servant had made it certain that she did not intend to act as nurse to dead men on their journey back to life. Reluctantly he decided to go without her.“How is it possible!” exclaimed the stranger, as they climbed into the waiting machine.“I have worked for fifteen years before I found the solution,” answered the professor slowly. “With electricity, all is possible; as I have proved. Electricity is the basis of every motive power we have; it is the base of every formation that we know.” 10. skeptical: doubtful “Go on,” said the stranger, “I am extremely interested.” 11. musing: thinking“Every sort of heat, whether dormant or active, is only one arm of the gigantic force, electricity. Most of our knowledge of electricity has been gained through its offspring, magnetism. A body entirely devoid of electricity is a body dead. Magnetism is apparent in many things including the human race, and its presence in many people is prominent.”“But how did this lead to your experiments?”“If magnetism or motive force is the offspring of electricity, the human body must, and does contain electricity. That we use more electricity than the human body will induce is a fact; it is apparent therefore that a certain amount of electricity must be generated within the human body, and without aid of any outside forces. Science has known for years that the body's power is brought into action through the brain. The brain is our generator. The little cells and the fluid that separate them, have the same action as the liquid of a wet battery; like a wet battery this fluid wears out and we must replace the fluid or the sal ammoniac or we lose the use of the battery or body. I have discovered what fluid to use that will produce the electricity in the brain cells which the human body is unable to induce.”“We are here,” said the stranger as he brought the car to a stop at the curb.The man led him into the house and introduced him to Mrs. Murray Attic, who conducted him to the room where the deceased Murray Attic was laid.Without a word the professor began his preparations. He was ill, and would have preferred to have been at rest in his own comfortable house. He would do the work quickly and get away.Selecting a gimlet12, he bored a hole through the skull of the dead man; inserting his hypodermic he injected all the fluid he had mixed. He had not calculated on the size of the gimlet and the dowels he carried would not fit the hole. As a last resource he drove in his lead pencil, broke it off close, and carefully cut the splinters smooth with the head.“It will be seventy-five cents, madam,” said the professor as he finished the work.Mrs. Murray Attic paid the money unconsciously; 12. gimlet: a small tool for boring hole she did not know whether he was embalming13 her 13. embalming: wrapping up in husband or just trying the keenness of his new tools. something in order to preserve The death had been too much for her.The minutes passed and still the dead man showed no signs of reviving. Professor Carbonic paced the floor in an agitated manner. He began to be doubtful of his ability to bring the man back. Worried, he continued his tramp14 up and down the room. His heart was affecting him. He was tempted to return the seventy-five cents to the prostrate wife when—THE DEAD MAN MOVED!? Do you think the professor is motivated The professor clasped his hands to his throat, more by helping others or by proving and with his head thrown back dropped to the 	his discovery? Explain. floor, a fatal attack of the heart.He became conscious quickly. “The bottles there,” he whispered. “Mix—, make injection.”He became unconscious again.	14. tramp: walkThe stranger found the gimlet and bored a hole in the professor's head; hastily seizing one of the vials he poured the contents into the deeply made hole. He then realized that there was another bottle.“Mix them!” shrieked the almost hysterical woman.It was too late, the one vial was empty, and the professor's body lay lifeless.In mental agony the stranger grasped the second vial and emptied its contents also into the professor's head, and stopped the hole with the cork.Miraculously Professor Carbonic opened his eyes, and rose to his feet. His eyes were like balls of fire; his lips moved inaudibly, and as they moved little blue sparks were seen to pass from one to another. His hair stood out from his head. The chemical reaction was going on in the professor's brain, with a dose powerful enough to restore ten men. He tottered15 slightly. Murray Attic, now thoroughly alive, sat up straight in bed. He grasped the brass bed post with one hand and stretched out the other to aid the staggering man.He caught his hand; both bodies stiffened; a slight crackling sound was audible; a blue flash shot from where Attic's hand made contact with the bedpost; then a dull thud as both bodies struck the floor. Both men were electrocuted16, and the formula is still a secret. 	– Jack G. HuekelsLearning 	Ask a science student, researcher or professor: Can a discovery that Through	seems helpful also become dangerous? Can you give a real example?InteractionJack G. HuekelsHe is an American chemist and educator who has taught at the high school and college levels. He has written several textbooks on chemistry, including Advanced Chemistry, which provides a comprehensive introduction to the subject. His work emphasizes the practical applications of chemistry and aims to make the subject accessible to a wide range of students.Story JourneyA scientist keeps 	He finally discovers a 	First, he revives a dead 	He then uses the experimenting in his 	formula that can bring 	rat and is overjoyed 	formula to bring an laboratory for many years	dead creatures back to life	by his success	injured girl back to lifeIn the end, a mistake with the formula 	The professor goes to revive 	Soon, more people causes disaster and both men die leaving 	a dead man, even though 	come to him hoping he the formula still a secret.	he is weak and unwell	can defeat death itselfRead the question carefully and darken the  circle of the correct answer in the OMR sheet. What has Professor Carbonic been working on for many years?A new machine 	(b) A magical potion  A scientific experiment to restore lifeA medicine for feverWhat is the first creature he successfully revives?A bird 	(b) A dog 	(c) A rat 	(d) A catWhat is the condition of the professor during the final experiment?Healthy 	(b) Very strong 	(c) Unwell 	(d) EnergeticWhat happens to the professor at the end of the story?He becomes famous   (b) He runs away  (c) He dies   (d) He becomes richWhat is Mag Nesia’s full name?Maggie Soda 	(b) Maggie Carbonic (c) Maggie Nesia 	(d) Martha NesiaComplete the sentences 	/Mag Nesia had been working with the professor for about ______ years.The professor carried his instruments in a __________________.The first human experiment was performed on _______________.Mag Nesia refused to assist later because she did not want to nurse _____________.The story suggests that science without caution can become _______________.C. Find lines from the story that tell you: Professor Carbonic was kind and humane.Professor Carbonic valued his duty more than his health.People were doubtful about the professor's discovery.The professor was honest in his dealings with people.The professor was given an overdose of the medicine by the stranger.Dr. Xray didn’t like anyone to interfere in his work.D. Answer the following questions  	CG:2, C:2.2	Critical Thinking‘Professor carbonic was diligently ……………… the "gie" on Maggie’? Write a short character sketch of the professor on the basis of the opening lines of the story.The old woman watched him, as she had done many times before, with always the same pitiful expression. Mag Nesia seldom expressed her views’. What is the reason behind this behaviour of the lady?Prof. Carbonic Makes a Great Discovery! What was the discovery?How did Mag Nesia react as the professor uttered the words ‘Great Saints!’?Describe the process the professor adopted to treat Sal Soda.Why was the professor hesitant to attend the case of the man who was killed in an accident?What happened to the professor as soon as he brought the dead man to life and why?	Put on Your Thinking Caps	 	/DiscussThe story supports the saying ‘Half knowledge is a dangerous thing’. Do you agree? Elaborate with reference to the climax.The same electricity that gave life, took life of the professor and Mr. Murray Attic. What proverb comes to your mind after reading the incident? Elaborate.The professor had to lose his life. Was he himself responsible for that or was there some other reason? Explain with reason.You read the story about a futuristic school where robots will replace the human teachers and students can study sitting in their room. What do you think about such an arrangement?	Dictionary & Vocabulary Skills	  	/A. Using your dictionary, list any five words that would appear between these guide words. 	Guide Words: chemistry – chlorine1. ___________________4. ___________________2. ___________________5. ___________________3. ___________________B. He sounded skeptical.Skeptical is a person who is doubtful of people or things around him. Write the correct word for each definition. Choose the word from the Help BoxWord Box amiable, frugal, vigilant, optimist, naive, rational 	 Word Definition 	1. ____________ someone who is logical and strategic 	2. ____________ someone who keeps careful watch for possible danger or difficulties. 	3. ____________ someone who is friendly, pleasant, and good-natured 	4. ____________ someone who spends money economically 	5. ____________ someone without wisdom or experience 	6. ____________ someone who is hopeful and confident	Grammar Lab	  	/Adverbs and their placementRead sentences from the story.The	old	woman	rolled	slowly	into	the	room.Professor	Carbonic	seated	himself	comfortably	and	awaited	the	action	of	his	injection.The coloured words in the above sentences are adverbs. You already know that adverbs are words that tell more about a verb.Here are some rules regarding the position of adverbs in a sentence.Adverbs of MannerExamples:  slowly, neatly, beautifully, fast, etc.These adverbs are put after the direct object or after the verb if there's no direct object.  subjectverb(s)direct objectadverbIplayedcricketwell.Iplayed well.Adverbs of PlaceExamples:  here, there, behind, after, outside, etc.Like adverbs of manner, these adverbs are put after the direct object or the verb.subjectverb(s)direct objectadverbShecalledmeinside.Iwentthere.Adverbs of TimeExamples:  yesterday, now, then, at night, etc.Adverbs of time are generally put at the end of the sentence.subjectverb(s)direct objectindirect objecttimeMy fatherwill sendmailto his managertomorrow.Adverbs of FrequencyExamples:  often, never, usually, etc.Adverbs of frequency are put directly before the main verb.subjectbeadverbmain verbobject, place or timeShe oftenvisitsthe orphanage.Ihavenevervisiteda hill station.A. Rewrite the sentences putting the adverbs in the correct position. 	1. I went on a long vacation. (last month) ________________________________  	2. She has terrible headaches. (often) 	 ________________________________  	3. The teacher explained the concept. (calmly) ________________________________  	4. Will you come? (upstairs) ________________________________  	5. I have tea without sugar. (always) ________________________________  	6. Most animals can see in the dark. (well)  ________________________________  	7. He was sleeping. (inside) ________________________________ 	Speaking Skills	 	CG:2, C:2.2	Critical ThinkingWork in pairs. Imagine that a student has invented a machine that can instantly complete all homework. One student is the inventor, and the other is a journalist interviewing the inventor.Prepare a dialogue of 6–8 exchanges. You may discuss:How the machine worksIts advantagesIts possible disadvantagesWhether students should use itPresent your dialogue before the class using proper voice modulation and expressions.	Writing Skills	  	CG:2, C:2.2	Critical ThinkingThe story is a piece of narrative writing that includes exposition (the beginning), rising action, climax, falling action, and resolution. The resolution is also called the denouement.Read the outlines of the story of Snow White Complete the story and mark the beginning, rising action, climax, falling action and resolution.Pretty princess.. queen dies giving birth ... .raised by the king and servants…wicked stepmother... orders to leave in forest to die… brother fakes her death…..saved by seven dwarfs ... .lives with them...stepmother comes to know...tries..to kill... first, with poisoned comb, then with poisoned apple…..fails to kill….dwarfs think she is dead...put her in a glass coffin...handsome Prince sees... kisses her...she revives...gets married...lives happily ever after.CompetenciesBeyondCG:2, C:2.2	 Interconnected Learning with Science  	Critical ThinkingClassify the following features into ‘Can be Artificially Controlled’ and ‘Cannot be Fully Controlled’.Feature	Category Heartbeat rhythm	______ Growth of cells	______ Movement of machines	______	Response to environment	______Scan & LearnScan the QR code to access Ask questions, clear yout the worksheet for this doubts, and learn more with chapter.AI.
